title: Scouring a raw fleece
subtitle: Washing the lanolin, vegetable matter, and grease from a freshly shorn fleece
excerpt: Scouring removes the lanolin and dirt from a raw fleece so it can be carded, combed, or dyed. The method is hot water, washing-up liquid, and gentle handling: no agitation, no temperature shock, no wringing. Done correctly, the fleece comes out clean, soft, and undamaged; done incorrectly, it felts into an unusable mat.
sourceNotes: Fleece scouring is fundamental to the wool preparation tradition. The hot-water, no-agitation method is standard practice as described in spinning guild manuals and Judith MacKenzie McCuin's preparation guides (in copyright, not quoted).

content.text: . Scouring removes the lanolin and grease so the fibre opens up for carding, combing, or dyeing. The critical rules are: hot water (60-65°C), no agitation, no temperature shock between baths. Breaking any of these three rules produces felt.

attrs.body: Carding and fleece handling throws fibre dust. Work in a ventilated room. Damp-mop after; never dry-sweep.
attrs.title: Wool dust

items.qty: as available; scour in batches of 200-300 g
items.name: Raw fleece
Washing-up liquid or specialist fibre wash
Mesh laundry bag or salad spinner

content.text: Fill the basin with hot water at 60-65°C and add washing-up liquid. Stir to dissolve. Lower the fleece in a mesh bag into the water, pressing it down gently until it is fully submerged. Do not agitate. Leave for 20 minutes. The water will turn yellow-brown as lanolin and grease dissolve.

Lift the bag out of the water and hold it over the basin to drain. Do not squeeze. Empty the dirty water and refill at the same temperature with clean hot water (no detergent). Lower the fleece back in and leave for 10 minutes to rinse. Repeat the rinse a second time.

Spin the bag in a salad spinner or washing machine (spin cycle only, no water) to remove excess moisture without agitation. Spread the fleece in a single layer on a rack or clean sheet and dry at room temperature or in a sunny spot. Turn once during drying. A fleece is ready to process when it is completely dry and pulls apart easily into locks.

items.fix: For the next batch: fill the basin before adding the fleece, lower in gently, do not stir or press, and lift straight out without any wringing.
items.cause: Agitation, temperature change, or pressure caused fibres to lock

items.fix: Scour in fresh hot water (60-65°C) with more detergent. A third bath is fine for a particularly greasy fleece.
items.cause: Water was too cool to melt the lanolin, or too little detergent was used
items.problem: Fleece still greasy or sticky after two scouring baths

items.fix: After drying, pick the fleece lock by lock over a waste bin, teasing each lock apart to release embedded matter before carding.
items.cause: Scouring removes loosely attached matter only; embedded seed heads require picking by hand
items.problem: Vegetable matter is still tangled throughout after washing
